title: Support running as a rake task without failing on error (ie: report-only)
author: jasonkarns
body:
Is there a way to run the rake task in a "report only" mode?

I'd like to include active_record_doctor in our collection of lint tasks, which are themselves kicked off via a primarily `lint` (locally) and `lint:ci` rake task (where each of the sub tasks are rake deps).

I'd love to include this task as one of the task deps on CI that would emit the report (as a to-do list, of sorts), but without failing the build.

The exit mode doesn't appear to be configurable?

Better yet, would be a way to generate the report in xunit format (or TAP) which can be consumed by more downstream tools and CI providers?

title: missing_presence_validation crashes with NoMethodError: undefined method 'exclude?' for Symbol
author: andy-rootly
body:
> Note: This issue was written with AI assistance.

## Description

`missing_presence_validation` crashes when a model uses an inclusion or exclusion validator where `:in` is a Symbol (i.e., a method name):

```ruby
validates :status, inclusion: { in: :allowed_statuses }
```

The detector calls `.exclude?` on the Symbol value, which fails because `Symbol` does not respond to `exclude?`.

## Error

```
NoMethodError: undefined method 'exclude?' for an instance of Symbol

                  (validator_items.is_a?(Proc) || validator_items.exclude?(nil))
                                                                 ^^^^^^^^^
```

## Root cause

In `lib/active_record_doctor/detectors/missing_presence_validation.rb`, `inclusion_or_exclusion_validator_items` returns `validator.options[:in]`, which can be a Symbol when the validator delegates to a method. The calling code on lines 96 and 103 assumes the result responds to `.exclude?` / `.include?`, which Symbols do not.

## Environment

- active_record_doctor 2.0.1
- Rails 8.0

title: Regex in ignore_attributes stopped working in v2.0
author: smathieu
body:
We have this configuration

```
  detector :missing_presence_validation,
    ignore_attributes: [
      /created_by/,
    ]
```

But we still get these errors:

```
add `optional: false` to Admin::AccExport.created_by - the foreign key created_by_id is NOT NULL
```

title: False-positive "the foreign key is NOT NULL"
author: xeron
body:
I have an app where registrations could be created by authenticated users, but also by guests without a user. I use `email` column as an optional `belongs_to`.

```ruby
class Registration < ApplicationRecord
  belongs_to :user, primary_key: :email, foreign_key: :email, inverse_of: :registrations, optional: true

  with_options presence: true do
    validates :email, format: { with: URI::MailTo::EMAIL_REGEXP }
  end
end
```

```ruby
class User < ApplicationRecord
  has_many :registrations, primary_key: :email, foreign_key: :email, inverse_of: :user, dependent: nil
end
```

After updating ARD to 2.0.0 I get the following suggestion which I believe is false-positive:
```
add `optional: false` to Registration.user - the foreign key user_id is NOT NULL
```

Several issues here:

1. There's no `user_id` column
2. `email` is `NOT NULL` because it's required
3. `belongs_to … optional: true` is because user with referenced email doesn't have to exist in DB

title: Please release v1.16.0
author: beetlegius-somos
body: Last release was on Aug 28, 2024, and there are [a lot of features added afterward](https://github.com/gregnavis/active_record_doctor/commits/master/) that would be nice to be available for us 😅

title: Suggestion: Require timestamp columns to have timezone
author: hlascelles
body:
By default, even with Rails 8, a new Rails installation will not use timestamps `with timezone` by default. They are (arguably wrongly) `without timezone`. There is some discussion of that here: https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/41084

It is agreed in that issue that it is Postgres best practice to use timezones, and Rails only uses `without timezone` defaults for legacy reasons. https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/53687

But, we can do better! If new installations won't make it the default, we can encourage it with a ARD check.

Would you accept a PR that checks to ensure timestamps are `with timezone`? It can have some helpful error info on how to make it the default:

```ruby
# config/application.rb
ActiveSupport.on_load(:active_record_postgresqladapter) do
  self.datetime_type = :timestamptz
end
```

title: Suggestion: Allow (or require) foreign keys to use BigInt even if the primary uses Integer
author: hlascelles
body:
Many of us have older codebases with primary keys as integers, not bigint. ARD allows these exceptions until you get round to migrating them.

What we don't want  is to allow the problem to propagate. I definitely don't want FKIDs to be `integer` when the primary is `bigint`, but I want to allow (force?) all new FKIDs to be be `bigint` regardless of the PKID type. This will the future big fix migration to concentrate on the primary tables only.

I am prepared to ignore for the (rare?, nearly impossible?) possibility of a very large number being put in the FKID column. That Shouldn't Happen.

Would you like a check for this, with the standard exceptions system for existing tables?

title: New detector to find excessive permissions on tables
author: hlascelles
body:
It may be tempting for new devs to set up your Rails instance with a single all powerful DB user, and use it for both migrations and runtime.

```sql
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO appuser;
```

This is, of course, bad practice from a least privilege point of view. At the very least have an ENV driven username that uses one power user for migrations/DDL, then a different runtime user for business logic.

Even then, the business logic user may have too many permissions.

We have a test which checks all tables and makes sure our app user cannot DELETE rows in any tables it is not supposed to.

The test is, roughly speaking:

```ruby
current_user =
  ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute("SELECT current_user").first["current_user"]

tables = ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute(<<~SQL).to_a
  SELECT table_name, table_schema
  FROM information_schema.tables
SQL

errors = tables.filter_map { |table|
  full_table_name = "#{table['table_schema']}.#{table['table_name']}"
  permissions = ActiveRecord::Base.connection.execute(<<~SQL).to_a
    SELECT has_table_privilege('#{current_user}', '#{full_table_name}', 'DELETE') AS can_delete
  SQL

  permissions.first["can_delete"] && full_table_name
}.compact
if errors.any?
  error_msg = errors.join("\n")
  fail "User #{current_user} has DELETE permission on some tables:\n#{error_msg}"
end
```

Would you see value in us adding a PR for ARD? There would have to be config to specify tables that allow DELETE. And probably expand the test to check for UPDATE too.

title: Global ignore is not always respected
author: vfonic
body:
I have some legacy models under `Legacy::` namespace. For example:

```ruby
class Legacy::Report < ApplicationRecord
  self.table_name = "legacy_nuovo_report"
  has_many :campioni_report, class_name: "Legacy::CampioniReport", foreign_key: "idreport", inverse_of: :report, dependent: :destroy

  validates :memo, length: { maximum: 65_535 }
end
```

Here's my `.active_record_doctor.rb` file:

```ruby
ActiveRecordDoctor.configure do
  global :ignore_tables, [/^legacy_/]
end
```

Here are some errors:

```
use `dependent: :delete_all` or similar on Legacy::Report.campioni_report - associated model Legacy::CampioniReport has no callbacks and can be deleted in bulk
the length validator on Legacy::Report.memo enforces a maximum of 65535 characters but there's no schema limit on legacy_nuovo_report.memo - remove the validator or the schema length limit
the schema limits legacy_report.campioni_qt to 255 characters but there's no length validator on Legacy::OldReport.campioni_qt - remove the database limit or add the validator
```

(the last one is a different model, but should still be ignored)

title: Suggest foreign keys only for columns with belongs_to
author: hatsu38
body:
## Summary
Currently, Active Record Doctor detects all columns ending with _id as missing foreign keys. However, not all _id columns require foreign key constraints. This leads to false positives in cases such as:

IDs used for external services (e.g., stripe_customer_id, slack_user_id)

https://github.com/gregnavis/active_record_doctor/blob/10411d43c4fe5d89f069f9388d1c959e90cf876a/lib/active_record_doctor/detectors/base.rb#L247-L252

On the other hand, detecting missing foreign keys based on belongs_to associations would provide a more accurate and meaningful result, aligning with ActiveRecord's data relationships.

## Potential Downside & Considerations
If a foreign key constraint is required but the model lacks a belongs_to association, it may not be detected.

However, in such cases, developers can resolve the issue by explicitly defining the belongs_to relationship.

Since _id-based detection results in excessive false positives, prioritizing belongs_to strikes a better balance between accuracy and usability.

Missing belongs_to associations in models can be seen as an issue beyond the scope of this gem.

title: Detector for tables without models
author: robinaugh
body:
Hi there @gregnavis, thanks for this gem! I've got a custom detector I'm running in our codebase to detect when a table is no longer referenced by a model. Is that something that would make sense to PR back to ActiveRecordDoctor, or is it outside of what you'd consider its core purpose?

For us, it's helpful to remember to drop a table after the ruby code using it has been removed, because the contents of the table are persisted in our data warehouse and it's safe to drop the production version of the table. However, the potential gotchas for others would be:
- If you don't back up the data elsewhere, you may _never_ want to drop a table, even if you phase out the model representing it
- The detector will flag a problem on the branch that removes the ruby code, but you shouldn't actually drop the table until that branch has been _deployed_, so you should always add it to the "ignored" list temporarily (or else do something dangerous).

It could also potentially be added to the gem but disabled by default with the caveats above documented.

Let me know what you think, again I'm happy to PR it. It's really just doing this:

```ruby
each_table(except: config(:ignore_tables)) do |table|
  matching_model = ActiveRecord::Base.descendants.find do |model|
    model.table_name == table
  end

  problem!(table:) if matching_model.nil?
end
```

title: Timestamps check should ignore ActiveStorage table
author: bmulholland
body:
On latest `main`:
```
add a created_at column to active_storage_variant_records
add a updated_at column to active_storage_variant_records
```

These are created/managed by ActiveStorage: https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_storage_overview.html#setup

So should be ignored out of the box for a good developer experience.

title: Rails 7.1+ Enum Validation
author: egreer
body:
Rails 7.1+ introduced validation on enums in https://github.com/rails/rails/pull/49100.

```rb
class Attachment < ApplicationRecord
  enum :attachment_type,
       { report: 'report', note: 'letter' },
       default: 'report',
       validate: true
       ...
```

When backed by a string the `incorrect_length_validation` raises:
```
the schema limits attachments.attachment_type to 255 characters but there's no length validator on Attachment.attachment_type - remove the database limit or add the validator
```

The `validates_inclusion_of` used under the hood by enum validation only accepts the options for `:allow_nil`, :allow_blank` and doesn't allow passing additional length validations.

I'd like a way to silence this without having to configure an ignore for each one. Or if possible check the enum options to ensure none of them have a length greater then the database col max when `validate: true` or `validate: { allow_nil: true }`

title: Checking the information in ignore_columns
author: Nymuxyzo
body: It would be advantageous for the developer if the Active Record Doctor were to point out two shortcomings. Firstly, if a column within ignore_columns does not even exist in the DB, or secondly, if the column corresponds to the rules of the respective detector, even if it occurs in ignore_columns.

title: False positives on missing_non_null_constraint for view-backed models
author: bmulholland
body: I've created a virtual join table, using a postgres view via scenic. I've set up a model that uses that view, setting relationships that belongs_to another object. When I do that, `missing_non_null_constraint` tells me the IDs should have a NOT NULL constraint in the DB, but of course that's invalid.

title: MySQL2 adapter shows false positive for case_insensitive_validations in Rails 7.2+
author: ndguarino
body:
When updating to Rails 7.2, Detector `missing_unique_indexes` returns problem `case_insensitive_validations` for columns despite expression indices not having support in the MySQL adapter. Root cause appears to be hardcoded version of 7.1 in the mysql check here: https://github.com/gregnavis/active_record_doctor/blob/c493c266fbc9540fec6c6f7bc2358a744a8bf82a/lib/active_record_doctor/utils.rb#L21

Can probably increase the version, but realistically it makes more sense to use the functionality in `ActiveRecord::ConnectionAdapters::AbstractAdapter#supports_expression_index?` added in Rails 5 which is specialized for each adapter (including SQLite, which includes a version check for older SQLite database versions which appears to be missing in utils.r as well).

title: missing_not_null_constraint error when validating presence only on create
author: ali-fetty
body:
Hello,

We have a use case where we need a record to be created with required fields, but these fields aren't required on updates.

Our validation setup looks like this:
`validates :city, presence: true, on: :create`

This column can still end up being null if the user deletes the value after it's initial creation. This leads to a missing_not_null_constraint error.

 Was this behavior intentional or is this a bug?

Thanks

title: `missing_presence_validation` incorrectly marks `numericaility` with `allow_nil: false` as missing a presence validator
author: vanharen
body:
first off, thanks for the gem!  very handy!

so, to be honest, i'm not 100% convinced this is really a bug, but figured i'd raise it, just in case it is.  please excuse me if this has been discussed previously -- i was unable to find any mention of it in issues.

so -- if you have a non-null integer field that has a validation like:
```
  validates :rank, numericality: {greater_than_or_equal_to: 0}, allow_nil: false
```

then it is my belief that this should suffice as a presence validator even though `presence: true` is not explicitly listed in the validator.  one *could* include this but it is redundant, imho, based on the following (from https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_record_validations.html#numericality)

> By default, numericality doesn't allow nil values. You can use allow_nil: true option to permit it. For Integer and Float columns empty strings are converted to nil.

so in the example above `allow_nil: false` should not really even be necessary as it is the default.  so the existence of the `numericality:` validator -- in the absence of `allow_nil: true` should suffice to be a presence validator for non-null integer columns, yes?

if my assertion is correct, i'd consider adding a PR to address this, once i get my fingers into the code.  i welcome discussion. thanks for reading!

title: Idea: timestamps (created_at, updated_at) should be NOT NULL
author: jdufresne
body:
Once the timestamps columns are established on a table, they might as well be NOT NULL to enforce their existence. All ActiveRecord operations set these values so they will always exist under normal usage.

Note: for fixing old tables, a project may need to backfill data with a migration.
